patches.find: No such comparison is presented. More fundamentally, the inter-city distances in this dataset are computed from WGS84 globe coordinates
patches.replace: No such comparison is presented. Note that the ellipse-vs-circle comparison is structurally a nested-model test: setting b/a = 1 collapses the ellipse to a circle, so an ellipse fit can never produce a worse RMS than a circle fit on the same data. The dome's "ellipse never lost to circle across V5–V9" is mathematically guaranteed by the model structure, not by any physical signal. More fundamentally, the inter-city distances in this dataset are computed from WGS84 globe coordinates
patches.rationale: Add nested-model triviality sentence (C2 recommendation not yet integrated). Makes explicit that the dome's 'ellipse wins' claim is mathematically inevitable when the ellipse subsumes the circle as a special case.

patches.find: so the ellipse is not discovering the Earth's shape — it is absorbing the radial scaling distortion that the Azimuthal Equidistant projection introduces when a sphere is projected onto a flat disc.
patches.replace: so the ellipse is not discovering the Earth's shape — it is absorbing the radial scaling distortion that the Azimuthal Equidistant projection introduces when a sphere is projected onto a flat disc. A defender may reply that we have presupposed WGS84 in our critique, but inter-city distances are independently confirmed by radar ranging, GPS time-of-flight, fiber-optic latency, and VLBI baseline interferometry — methods that are not geometrically committed to a globe shape yet converge on the same inter-city distances as WGS84 computation.
patches.rationale: Preempt the WGS84-circularity defense prong (defense_survives=3 in C3 review). Multi-method convergence on inter-city distances is an independent line of evidence not dependent on WGS84 presupposition.
